DPDP Act, 2023 PM Case Study Spotify India

Consent that's
specific enough to trust
and simple to use.

A complete redesign of Spotify India's privacy & consent experience — bringing onboarding, ongoing permissions, and data rights into alignment with India's DPDP Act, 2023, without taxing the engagement the product depends on.

Executive Summary

A trust product, not just a compliance one

Spotify's current India flow bundles all data collection behind one sign-up checkbox, personalisation on by default, with no in-app record of what was agreed to and no self-serve way to exercise data rights. This case study redesigns that experience end to end.

₹250 Cr
Maximum DPDP penalty per non-compliance instance
1 tap
Today's bundled consent for personalisation + ads + analytics
≥ 92%
Target Consent Health Rate — the North Star metric
13
Functional requirements spanning onboarding to Kids controls

Business Problem

Bundled, non-itemised consent and the absence of self-serve data rights create direct regulatory exposure under DPDP, and an avoidable trust liability in a market where Spotify competes on user goodwill as much as catalogue.

User Problem

Users can't see what's collected, can't easily say no to parts of it, and can't withdraw consent as easily as they gave it — and parents have no verified say in what's collected from their children.

Product Opportunity

Done right, a well-designed consent experience reduces support burden, strengthens retention among privacy-conscious users, and positions Spotify ahead of competitors still running bundled consent in India.

A

Aditi, 27

Free + Premium listener
"Let me enjoy personalised music without babysitting settings."
R

Rehan, 16

Teen user
"Give me real controls, not a watered-down app."
M

Meera, 41

Parent, Family Plan owner
"Let me approve once and trust the defaults after."
K

Kabir, 9

Child, Spotify Kids
"Just play the song when I tap it."

Product Thinking

The 10-slide narrative behind the design

Problem framing, personas, a DPDP audit of today's flow, the Consent Hub concept, metrics, a live diagnostic scenario, and the rollout plan — presented as it was built.

Slide 1 / 10

Consent Is Spotify's Weakest Skip Button

Focus: onboarding permissions & ongoing personalisation consent for Indian users, under the DPDP Act, 2023.

  • Legal exposure — penalties reach ₹250 Cr per instance; India is Spotify's 2nd-largest market by MAU.
  • Trust erosion — today's flow bundles personalisation, ads and analytics into one all-or-nothing "Allow" tap.
  • Children's data gap — Family Plan & Kids collect listening + voice data without verifiable parental consent.
  • Product opportunity — a trustworthy, granular consent layer can become a retention and conversion lever.
Slide 2 / 10

Four People, Four Very Different Consent Needs

Aditi, 27 — Free+PremiumDoesn't know mic access powers voice search, not eavesdropping.
Rehan, 16 — TeenAd personalisation feels invasive; unsure what parents can see.
Meera, 41 — ParentNo visibility into what data her child's listening generates.
Kabir, 9 — ChildCannot meaningfully consent; needs zero decision friction.
Slide 3 / 10

Auditing Today's Flow Against DPDP Expectations

  • Account creation — self-declared birth year only → no verifiable age-check, minors can pass as adults.
  • Personalisation — bundled into "Agree & Continue" → fails purpose limitation.
  • Microphone — framed vaguely as "voice features" → notice doesn't name the specific purpose.
  • Advertising — on by default → no easy "reject" equal in prominence to "accept".
  • Children — child profile inherits data collection → no verifiable parental consent event captured.
Slide 4 / 10

The Privacy & Consent Hub

One native surface inside Settings that makes consent granular, reversible and legible — without adding steps to first play.

Granular purpose togglesPersonalisation, analytics, ads & location — independent switches.
Consent history & renewalEvery grant/withdrawal timestamped; auto-expires after 12 months.
Data minimisation defaultsNon-essential toggles ship OFF by default.
Verifiable parental consentOTP + light ID check before any minor's profile collects data.
Slide 5 / 10

End-to-End Consent Journey

  • 1 · Onboarding — core playback works with zero optional data
  • 2 · Granular consent — one-screen toggles, plain-language purposes
  • 3 · Personalise — recs improve live as toggles turn on
  • 4 · Manage anytime — Consent Hub, always one tap away
  • 5 · Parent verifies — OTP + ID check unlocks child profile features
  • 6 · Withdraw / delete — effective within 72 hours

Design principle: consent collection never blocks first play. Only Manage and Withdraw/Delete are user-initiated.

Slide 6 / 10

Metrics & Success Criteria

North Star: Consent Health Rate (CHR) — % of MAUs in India with valid, current, purpose-level consent on record.

CHR≥ 85% within 90 days
Consent completion≥ 92%
Deletion SLA≤ 72 hrs, 99% of requests
Complaints< 0.05% of MAU / month
Slide 7 / 10

CHR Drops 15% in Week 3 — Now What?

No major release shipped. Walking the hypothesis tree before touching the roadmap:

  • Product/technical — consent-expiry job mis-fired; SDK update reset local toggle state.
  • External/policy — MeitY draft rules tightened a definition; OS-level prompt now double-gates.
  • Segment-specific — concentrated in one OS, region, age band, or cohort?

Action plan: slice CHR by platform/version/cohort → check expiry-cron logs → pull support & app-store themes → hotfix if isolated, pause rollout if broad.

Slide 8 / 10

Privacy & Consent Health Dashboard

Consent Health Rate86.4% · +2.1 pts WoW
Consent Completion93.2% · +0.4 pts WoW
Deletion SLA Met99.1% · flat
Active Complaints0.03% · -0.01 pts WoW

Insight: Android + Free + 18-24 is the weakest segment (79.2% CHR) — points to a prompt/placement issue in free-tier nudges, not a policy gap.

Slide 9 / 10

Rollout Plan & Risk Matrix

Dogfood (2 wks) → QA + legal sign-off (1 wk) → Beta, 5% opt-in (3 wks) → India rollout, staged by platform → Global template for GDPR/CCPA.

Not A/B tested: parental verification and purpose-limitation defaults — these are compliance floors. Only surrounding UX (copy, timing, hierarchy) is experimented on.

Slide 10 / 10

Key Decisions, Trade-offs & What's Next

  • Settings-native Hub, not a modal — keeps consent discoverable, not a one-time interruption.
  • Defaults OFF for everything non-essential — trades initial personalisation for valid, DPDP-aligned consent.
  • OTP-first parental verification, ID check as fallback only — rejected pure self-declaration and mandatory ID-upload.

Next 2 quarters: Q1 — Consent Hub GA, parental verification, data export/delete. Q2 — consent-aware recommendations; extend Hub to GDPR/US-state markets.

1 / 10

Product Requirements Document

Thirteen requirements, specified end to end

Search or jump to any section. Every requirement below carries its priority, user story, acceptance criteria, edge cases, and success metric — exactly as authored.

Download the PRD (.docx) Open in new tab
Conceptual System Overview

What the product needs to exist — not how engineering builds it

This is the PRD's own Section 7–8 content: a product-planning view to align design, engineering, and legal on scope. It intentionally stops short of service boundaries, data stores, or API design — those are engineering's call, not a PM's to invent.

By design, not by omission: the source PRD explicitly excludes invented database schemas, microservice diagrams, and API specifications — noting that presenting fabricated engineering architecture would misrepresent what a PM role owns. This section mirrors that boundary faithfully.

Client experience

Onboarding, Dashboard, and Kids-control surfaces on iOS, Android, and Web (FR-01–FR-07, FR-12).

Consent recording

System of record for what each user has currently agreed to, per purpose (FR-03, FR-05, FR-06).

Consent history

An append-only log of every change, independent of current state (FR-07).

Rights fulfillment

Handling for download and deletion requests, including status tracking (FR-08, FR-09).

Parental verification

OTP and ID-check flow gating Kids profile creation (FR-11).

Notification triggers

Renewal reminders, export-ready alerts, deletion confirmations (FR-08, FR-09, FR-13).

Illustrative flow — Consent capture

User completes onboarding, sets permissions on-device
→
Client sends permission set + summary version on Accept
→
Consent recording writes state + history in one transaction
→
Dashboard & features read consent recording as source of truth

Illustrative flow — Deletion request

User confirms deletion from the Dashboard
→
Rights fulfillment logs request & status, notifies user
→
Deletion executes across all systems within the target window
→
Completion logged to consent history & confirmed to user

DPDP Requirement Mapping

Every DPDP theme, addressed by a specific feature

A general, non-legal reading of the Act's themes for product-planning purposes — validated against Privacy Counsel's review before launch, not a substitute for it.

Metrics Dashboard

Consent Health Rate is the North Star

The percentage of active users with valid, current, purpose-specific consent on file — tracked alongside guardrails that must never regress: retention and Premium conversion.

Segmentation — this week

SegmentCHRWithdrawal

Insight: Android + Free + 18–24 is the weakest segment — a prompt/placement issue in free-tier nudges, not a policy or legal gap.

Rollout & Roadmap

Staged rollout, compliance floors first

Core consent gates — parental verification, purpose-limitation defaults — are never A/B tested; they're compliance floors. Only surrounding UX is experimented on.

Phase 1

Dogfood — 2 weeks

Internal Spotify India employees exercise the full flow before any external exposure.

Phase 2

QA + legal sign-off — 1 week

DPO and compliance review against the DPDP requirement mapping.

Phase 3

Beta — 3 weeks

5% of India users, opt-in cohort, before wider exposure.

Phase 4

India rollout

100% of India, staged by platform to isolate any regressions quickly.

Phase 5

Global template

Adapt the Consent Hub for GDPR and CCPA markets.

Q1

Consent Hub GA in India

Parental verification and data export/delete ship alongside general availability.

Q2

Consent-aware recommendations

The recommendation engine respects per-purpose opt-outs in real time; Hub template extends to EU and US-state markets.

Risk matrix

Med likelihoodHigh impact

Consent-step friction drops Premium conversion.

Mitigation: progressive disclosure; no gate before first play.
High likelihoodMed impact

Engineering effort retrofitting legacy SDK data flows.

Mitigation: phase by data category; ship minimisation first.
Med likelihoodHigh impact

Parental verification abandonment.

Mitigation: OTP-first, ID check only as fallback, never default.
Low likelihoodMed impact

Users mass-withdraw analytics consent.

Mitigation: explain the value exchange in plain language, in context.

About This Project

Why this case study exists

  • Vision A Spotify where giving up personal data feels like a choice, not a toll for entry — consent that's specific enough to trust and simple enough to actually use.
  • Scope discipline The PRD explicitly stops at what a PM should own — behavior and outcomes — and flags where engineering, legal, and security ownership begins, rather than presuming to author that work.
  • Design process Grounded in a persona-first audit of the current India flow against DPDP's specific themes: purpose limitation, itemised notice, verifiable parental consent, and the right to withdraw as easily as to give.
  • Key trade-off Defaults OFF for everything non-essential trades a lower initial personalisation rate for consent that is actually valid — chosen deliberately over the alternative of nudging users toward "Accept All."
